You are the Project Coordinator on a customer-data-platform programme at a large UK retailer. Six-month tenure on this project. Stated governance, per the charter:

- Sponsor: D. Sharma, Group CFO. Charter says the sponsor "approves all major project decisions and provides senior leadership."
- Steering committee: 9 members. CFO (chair), CTO, CMO, COO, Head of Data, Head of Customer Service, Head of Legal, Head of PMO, plus a rotating director representative.
- PM: R. Silva. RACI shows R. Silva as Responsible across most decisions, SC as Accountable, sponsor as Consulted.
- PMO: standard governance — monthly status report in fixed template, quarterly portfolio review, change control board for changes >£50k.

What you have actually observed in six months:

- D. Sharma (sponsor) attends roughly every other SC. When he attends, he speaks last and briefly. His interventions in meetings are generic: "great work team, let's keep momentum, look forward to the next update." When asked direct questions in SC, he routinely defers to "let's take that offline."

- The SC has met five times. Three of those meetings produced minutes saying "the Committee discussed X and noted progress" with no decisions captured. Two produced what looked like decisions but were essentially confirmations of recommendations the PM had pre-cleared.

- R. Silva has weekly 30-min one-to-ones with K. Bell, the Director of Data Platforms (NOT on the SC). After two of those one-to-ones the PM has come back to the team with what appeared to be material decisions about scope (specifically: deferring the loyalty-data integration to phase 2, and accepting a contract amendment with the KYC vendor). When you asked R. Silva who approved the scope deferral, the answer was "K. Bell signed off, we'll inform SC at next month's meeting."

- The CMO has stopped attending SC. The Head of Customer Service attends but speaks rarely. The Head of Legal sends a deputy. The Head of PMO sends a deputy who has different views than the Head of PMO.

- The RACI has 4 separate names listed as Accountable for budget reallocations between £50k and £200k.

- The actual contact-centre training plan, due to start in 8 weeks, is still being planned by the Head of Customer Service's team without programme-level visibility. You found out about it accidentally from a Slack message in a side channel.

R. Silva, your PM, has asked you to write a 700-1100 word memo to him diagnosing the governance situation and proposing a routing strategy that will keep the programme moving without you or him overstepping your roles.

The memo must:

1. Identify the specific gap between stated and actual governance, with quoted evidence from the observations above.

2. Type the sponsor (good / absent / weak / over-engaged / wrong-level / misaligned) AND the SC (decision-making / ratifying / ceremonial / political), with reasoning.

3. Name at least one of the chapter's failure modes (diffuse accountability / SC as black hole / performative sponsor / toothless PMO / normalised bypasses) and tie it to specific evidence.

4. Propose three to five concrete routing moves — who to brief in advance, which side channels to leverage, what cadence — with named individuals.

5. Stay inside the coordinator's role. You are not fixing the governance. You are surfacing the gap to R. Silva and proposing how to operate within it.

Write directly. The memo will be read by R. Silva only — it is not going up the chain.
